cards.text: Any substance that is used to create an object is referred to as material.
New Class Excitement Ghulan and Sheeta are excited to begin a new class session after the long summer holiday.
Daily Life Materials Madam Vidya engages students in discussions about common items they use daily.
Examination of Objects Students are asked to consider shapes, colors, weight, and touch sensations of daily objects.
Material Definition Objects are made from materials such as paper, wood, cloth, and metal.
Historical Pottery Overview Pottery in the Indian subcontinent dates back 7,000-8,000 years.
Early Pottery Locations Lahuradewa and Mehrgarh are significant archaeological sites for early pottery.

Development of Techniques Post 4000 BCE pottery in the Sindhu-Sarasvatī region involved advanced techniques:
  • Wheel-turned production
  • Use of pigmentation
  • Protective decorative coats (slips)
  • Decorative painting
Sophisticated Pottery in the Harappan Civilisation Pottery featured bright red surfaces painted with black geometric patterns and animal designs.
Clay Preparation The clay was meticulously cleaned, sieved, and kneaded before wheel-turning and kiln-baking.
Pottery Types and Uses Pottery items included pots, dishes, and bowls, used for cooking and storing grains, oil, and ghee.
Terracotta Characteristics Baked clay pots are known as terracotta items.
Exhibition of Historical Pottery Large storage jars and pottery are displayed at the National Museum in New Delhi.
Utility and Craft of Pots Pots had multifunctional purposes, extending from culinary to storage roles.
Pottery and Cultural Heritage The sophistication in pottery reflects the rich cultural heritage and advancement of the Harappan Civilisation.
Importance of Material Understanding Studying materials and their properties helps understand historical crafting methods and daily object uses.
The method of arranging the objects into groups is called classification. Objects can be classified on the basis of a common property that they have.

Classification
The arrangement of objects into groups is known as classification. It is based on a common property shared by the objects.

Common Property for Classification
When classifying objects, the common property that they possess is crucial for determining how they are grouped.

Tumbler Material Requirement
The materials used to construct a tumbler need to possess certain qualities. They must be able to hold water effectively.

Properties of Suitable Tumbler Materials
  • Water-resistant
  • Durable
  • Non-porous
  • Safe for holding liquids

Examples of Tumbler Materials
  • Plastic
  • Glass
  • Metal
  • Stainless Steel
These materials are chosen based on their ability to hold water securely.

topics.topic: What are the different Properties of Materials?

cards.text: Materials that typically have shiny surfaces are said to have a lustrous appearance. Such materials with lustre are usually metals. Examples of metals include iron, copper, zinc, aluminium, gold, etc.
Non-lustrous materials are those that do not have a shiny surface. Some examples of non-lustrous materials are paper, wood, rubber, jute, etc.
The materials, through which things can be seen clearly, are called transparent. Glass, water, air, cellophane paper, etc.
There are many materials through which you are not able to see at all. These materials are called opaque. Wood, cardboard and metals are examples of opaque materials.
The materials through which objects can be seen, but not clearly, are known as translucent. Butter paper and frosted glass are examples of translucent materials.
Some materials completely disappear when mixed in water. We say that these materials dissolve in water or, in other words, they are soluble in water.
Some materials do not mix with water and do not disappear even after we stir them for a long time. These materials are insoluble in water.
Any object which is heavier or lighter can be measured in terms of a property called mass.

Appearance of Materials
Materials differ in appearance, such as freshly cut wood versus iron, or copper versus aluminum. Observing texture, color, and luster can help identify and classify materials based on their visual characteristics.

Sorting Challenge
Collect and observe small pieces of various materials like paper, wood, and metals. Note their shine, texture, and other features and group them by appearance. Metals like iron and copper usually exhibit luster but can lose it due to environmental effects.

Lustrous vs Non-Lustrous Materials
Materials with a shiny surface are lustrous, typically metals. Non-lustrous materials do not shine and include paper and wood. Not all shiny materials are metals, as polish or coatings can enhance their appearance.

Material Hardness
Material hardness varies based on how easily they can be compressed or scratched. Hard materials resist compression and scratching, whereas soft materials do not. Hardness is relative, with rubber being softer than iron but harder than sponge.

Transparent Materials
Transparent materials, like glass or water, allow clear viewing through them. Examples include air and cellophane paper. These materials are crucial in applications requiring visibility, such as windows or containers.

Opaque Materials
Opaque materials block light and prevent visibility through them. Common examples include wood, cardboard, and metals, often used for privacy or shielding applications.

Translucent Materials
Translucent materials, like frosted glass and butter paper, allow light to pass through but distort vision. They are used for decorative purposes where partial visibility is desired.

Solubility in Water
Solubility determines if a material can dissolve in water. Soluble materials, like sugar and salt, disappear in water, whereas insoluble materials do not mix despite stirring.

Insoluble Materials
Materials like oils do not mix with water and form separate layers when left undisturbed. Certain gases, such as oxygen, dissolve in water and are vital for aquatic life.

Heavy or Light Materials
Weight measures material mass, with heavier materials having more mass. Mass is often measured in grams or kilograms, impacting transportation and usage.

Space and Volume
Volume measures the space a substance occupies, as seen with water levels in a glass. Different volumes indicate varying amounts of material, crucial in measurement and utilization.

Effects of Air and Moisture
Metals may appear dull due to exposure to air and moisture, affecting their luster. Freshly cut surfaces may reveal their original shine, emphasizing environmental impacts on materials.

Relative Hardness
Hardness is relative; rubber is harder than sponge but softer than metal. This property influences selection for particular uses depending on durability and resilience requirements.

Transparent vs. Translucent Materials
Ghulan's game illustrated transparency where clear visibility occurs and translucency with hazy visibility. Matching material type to usage requirements ensures suitable application.

Capacity and Volume Distinction
Identical containers can hold different amounts based on the volume of contents. Volume labels on products, like bottles, indicate capacity for consumer understanding.
Anything that occupies space and has mass is called matter. The mass gives the quantity of matter, and the units to measure it are gram (g) and kilogram (kg).
The space occupied by matter is its volume. The units to measure the volume are litre (L) and millilitre (mL).

Definition of Matter

Matter is anything that occupies space and has mass. It consists of materials with properties.

Properties of Matter

All materials possess two fundamental properties: mass and volume.

Mass of Matter
  • Mass gives the quantity of matter.
  • Measured in grams (g) and kilograms (kg).
  • SI unit for mass is kilogram (kg).
  • Always leave a space between the numerical value and the unit, e.g., 7 kg.

Volume of Matter
  • Space occupied by matter is called volume.
  • Measured in litres (L) and millilitres (mL).
  • SI unit for volume is cubic metre (m3).
  • 1 m3 equals 1000 L.

Unit Abbreviations
  • Kilogram abbreviated as kg with no space between 'k' and 'g'.
  • Litre abbreviated as capital L; millilitre as mL with no space between 'm' and 'L'.

Materials and Objects
  • Materials are types of matter used to create objects.
  • Objects can be made from single or multiple materials.

Classification of Materials
  • Classification groups objects based on properties.
  • Examples include grouping rocks, plants, and animals.

Ayurveda Classification

Ayurveda uses 20 properties (guṇa) in the classification of materials, described in the shloka from Aṣhtanga hṛidaya.

Non-Living World Classification

Non-living entities are classified based on shared properties like the living world.

Properties for Classification
  • Appearance: lustrous vs. non-lustrous.
  • Texture: hard vs. soft.
  • Transparency: transparent, translucent, or opaque.
  • Solubility: soluble vs. insoluble.

Use of Properties

Properties of materials determine their practical use and group classification.

Mass and Volume Relationship

Mass quantifies matter's amount, while volume is the space it occupies.

Grouping Based on Similarities and Differences
  • Materials with similar properties are grouped together.
  • Dissimilar materials are grouped based on distinct characteristics.

Practical Application
  • Classification aids in determining material suitability.
  • Diverse materials can have similar uses.

Summary Points
  • Classification is crucial for understanding material properties.
  • Mass and volume are central to defining matter.
Objects are made from a large variety of materials. An object can be made up of a single material or a combination of different materials.
We can use different materials to make objects with similar functions.
The method of arranging objects into groups is called classification.
Materials possess different properties which determine their use.
Materials are grouped or classified based on their similarities or differences in their properties.
Materials can be grouped based on appearance, such as lustrous or non-lustrous and based on the feel, such as hard or soft.
Materials are grouped as transparent, translucent or opaque depending on how much we can see through them.
Mass quantifies the amount of matter present in an object.
